sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model-based RLHF fits a preference predictor and optimizes a policy against it, usually with a divergence penalty limiting drift from a reference model.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang arXiv abstract lines 38-40 describes collecting output rankings and further fine-tuning a supervised model using RLHF; DPO arXiv abstract lines 42-44 describes RLHF first fitting a reward model reflecting human preferences and then using RL while avoiding drift from the original model.
sentence_reviews.notes: The definition is directly supported by the RLHF and DPO abstracts.

sentence_reviews.text: The role of Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF is to connect Reward modeling, Policy-gradient & actor–critic objectives to downstream capabilities including Conversational assistants & chatbots.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang lines 37-40 supports language models following user intent via supervised fine-tuning and RLHF; DPO lines 42-45 supports reward modeling and RLHF policy optimization. Conversational assistants and RLAIF are not directly named in these abstracts.
sentence_reviews.notes: Reward modeling and policy optimization are supported; chat assistant downstream role is inferred from instruction-following/user-intent setting.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang arXiv abstract lines 37-40 discusses aligning language models with user intent over prompts and desired model behavior, but does not explicitly use the phrase chatbot.
sentence_reviews.notes: The chat-assistant connection is highly plausible but not exact in the inspected abstract.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: DPO arXiv abstract lines 42-44 describes RLHF first fitting a reward model reflecting human preferences; Ouyang lines 38-40 describes ranking outputs and RLHF.

sentence_reviews.text: Results are sensitive to data quality, hyperparameters, compute budget, random variation, and evaluation contamination. Its direct dependencies include Reward modeling, Policy-gradient & actor–critic objectives.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: DPO arXiv abstract line 44 calls RLHF complex and often unstable, with reward-model fitting and RL fine-tuning; it does not directly support every generic sensitivity clause.
sentence_reviews.notes: Instability and dependencies are supported; data quality/randomness/contamination clauses need more specific sources.

sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF is learned, selected, or evaluated using the objective family Reward modeling.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: DPO arXiv abstract lines 42-44 describes RLHF fitting a reward model reflecting human preferences; Ouyang lines 38-40 describes rankings used to further fine-tune via RLHF.
sentence_reviews.notes: Reward modeling is central to reward-model-based RLHF.

sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF is learned, selected, or evaluated using the objective family Policy-gradient & actor–critic objectives.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: DPO arXiv abstract lines 43-45 contrasts DPO with RLHF that fine-tunes the LM using reinforcement learning to maximize reward; Ouyang lines 38-40 says RLHF further fine-tunes the supervised model.
sentence_reviews.notes: RL policy optimization is directly supported; actor-critic specifically is not named in the inspected abstracts.

sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF uses Supervised fine-tuning & instruction tuning as a reusable ingredient in its defined operation.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang arXiv abstract line 38 describes fine-tuning GPT-3 using supervised learning before collecting rankings and applying RLHF.
sentence_reviews.notes: Directly supported for the InstructGPT/RLHF pipeline.

sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF uses Preference learning as a reusable ingredient in its defined operation.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang lines 38-40 uses rankings of model outputs; DPO lines 42-44 describes human labels of relative quality and reward models reflecting preferences.

sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF is learned, selected, or evaluated using the objective family Process supervision & step-level reward models.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang supports reward-model RLHF generally; Snell arXiv abstract lines 44-45 discusses searching against process-based verifier reward models at test time; no inspected source establishes process reward models as common for RLHF/RLAIF broadly.
sentence_reviews.notes: Process reward models are related but not directly a common dependency for reward-model RLHF as stated.

sentence_reviews.text: Reward-model training, RLHF & RLAIF is learned, selected, or evaluated using the objective family Outcome verifiers & executable rewards.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: RLVR arXiv abstract lines 41-44 discusses verifiable rewards for LLM reasoning, answer correctness, and reasoning quality; it does not directly cover standard reward-model RLHF/RLAIF.
sentence_reviews.notes: Outcome verifiers are relevant to verifiable-reward RL but optional and not direct evidence for reward-model RLHF.

edge_reviews.notes: RLHF reward model fitting is directly supported; core strength is correct.
RL policy optimization is supported, but actor-critic is a narrower implementation detail not directly named; core strength for policy-gradient/RL objective family is defensible.
Supervised fine-tuning precedes RLHF in the inspected InstructGPT pipeline; common strength is supported.
Rankings/relative-quality labels directly support preference learning as a common ingredient.
Process reward models are related to verifier-guided search/test-time compute, but the inspected evidence does not support them as a common RLHF dependency.
Verifiable rewards support optional related RL training, but not reward-model RLHF specifically.
